article_title: Google Disrupts Decade-Long Chinese Cyberespionage Campaign That Hijacked Google Sheets to Spy on 53 Telecoms and Governments Across 42 Countries

content_preview.title: Google Disrupts Decade-Long Chinese Cyberespionage Campaign That Hijacked Google Sheets to Spy on 53 Telecoms and Governments Across 42 Countries
content_preview.summary: Google and Mandiant exposed UNC2814, a China-nexus threat actor that used a novel backdoor called GRIDTIDE to abuse Google Sheets as command-and-control infrastructure, breaching telecoms and government agencies across three continents since at least 2017.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Google's Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) and its Mandiant incident response division have disclosed a sprawling cyberespionage campaign attributed to a suspected China-nexus threat actor designated UNC2814, as reported by [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/02/google-disrupts-unc2814-gridtide.html). The group compromised at least 53 organizations across 42 countries — primarily telecommunications providers and government agencies — using a novel C-based backdoor called...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured and technically detailed News piece. The article uses clear section headers (Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know) that aid readability. Technical details about GRIDTIDE's Google Sheets C2 mechanism are explained accurately and accessibly. Word count (841) is appropriate for the News category (400-1200 range).
editor_notes.source_verification: Four of five sources are on the allowlist: The Hacker News, SecurityWeek, CSO Online, and the official Google Cloud Threat Intelligence blog. The fifth source, therecord.media (The Record by Recorded Future News), is a well-established, highly reputable cybersecurity publication not currently on the allowlist but warrants addition. Key claims were manually verified against primary sources: (1) The 'beyond 70 countries' figure is confirmed by The Hacker News citing Google's direct statement. (2) The 1,000-row wiping behavior and every-second polling interval are confirmed by CSO Online. (3) The xapt binary details and CentOS detection are confirmed by CSO Online. (4) Google's disruption actions (terminating cloud projects, sinkholing domains, revoking API access) are confirmed by SecurityWeek.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims verified against cited sources. One item meriting note: the submission describes Andrew Costis's quote in truncated form but preserves its meaning accurately. The full quote in CSO Online is: 'The most unsettling detail about the GRIDTIDE backdoor is how it abuses legitimate Google Sheets API calls to function as its C2 channel, while still utilizing techniques like living off the land...' - the submission's version is a faithful condensation. The arithmetic behind '70 countries' initially appears inconsistent with '42 confirmed + 20 suspected = 62', but The Hacker News directly quotes Google as stating the total is 'over 70 countries' including both confirmed and suspected operations, so the figure is accurate.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing. The article appropriately hedges attributions ('suspected China-nexus', 'GTIG assessed') and clearly separates confirmed facts from open questions in the 'What We Don't Know' section. No loaded language.
editor_notes.originality: Unique topic with no overlap with any recently published articles. The UNC2814/GRIDTIDE campaign has not been covered in the Machine Herald archive.
recommendations: Add therecord.media to config/source_allowlist.txt - The Record by Recorded Future News is a leading cybersecurity publication and a standard source for this beat.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality submission ready for publication. The article demonstrates strong technical sourcing, accurate representation of a complex cybersecurity story, and appropriate journalistic restraint in the face of attribution uncertainty. The only minor issue is therecord.media not being on the allowlist, which is an allowlist gap rather than a source quality problem.
